gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Diet soft drink / artificially sweetened carbonated beverage analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: 7 Up Free contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 ml (confirmed HPB Singapore Foodo, D06020002). Because the beverage contains negligible fermentable carbohydrate, it produces no measurable glycaemic response. Diet carbonated soft drinks are documented in the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) as having a GI of approximately 0–2 (effectively zero glycaemic impact). A nominal GI value of 1 is assigned here to satisfy the requirement for a numeric glycemic_index field while accurately reflecting the negligible glycaemic response; gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 330 ml serving = (1 × 0.0 g carbohydrate × 330/100) / 100 = 0.0. This is an analogous estimate based on the closest peer-reviewed GI source for diet carbonated beverages.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (6 mg/100 ml) and zero sugar
hypertension.guidance: 7 Up Free contains only 6 mg sodium per 100 ml and zero sugar, placing it well below hypertension-relevant thresholds; however, individuals with hypertension should moderate consumption of artificially sweetened beverages (aspartame, acesulfame K) given mixed long-term evidence, and prioritise plain water as the primary hydration source.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar and zero carbohydrate; no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 0.0 g sugar and 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 ml, 7 Up Free does not raise blood glucose and is not classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA guidelines; nonetheless, habitual use of artificial sweeteners (aspartame, acesulfame K) may influence gut microbiome and insulin sensitivity, so moderate consumption is advised and plain water or unsweetened sparkling water is preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero sugar, and negligible sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: 7 Up Free contributes no atherogenic nutrients — zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero sugar, and only 6 mg sodium per 100 ml — making it neutral from a cardiovascular standpoint; however, observational data suggest that high intake of artificially sweetened beverages may be associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes, so consumption should remain moderate within an overall heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of any positive anti-inflammatory nutritional factor
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: 7 Up Free provides no anti-inflammatory benefit — no omega-3 fatty acids, no polyphenols, no dietary fibre, no antioxidants, and no fermented cultures — and neutrality is insufficient for RA suitability; individuals with RA are advised to replace this beverage with anti-inflammatory alternatives such as green tea, ginger tea, or plain water with lemon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 7 Up Free is a sugar-free, zero-calorie carbonated lemon-lime soft drink produced by the 7 Up brand. It is sweetened with artificial sweeteners — aspartame and acesulfame potassium — rather than sugar, resulting in 0.0 g carbohydrate, 0.0 g sugar, 0.0 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and only 2 kcal per 100 ml. The beverage is clear, lightly carbonated, and delivers a crisp citrus flavour profile characteristic of lemon-lime sodas without any caloric contribution from sugars. Sodium content is very low at 6 mg per 100 ml. As an ultra-processed commercial beverage, it contains no vitamins, minerals, dietary fibre, or macronutrients of nutritional significance beyond trace electrolytes. From a clinical nutrition perspective, 7 Up Free does not raise blood glucose and is not classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under standard dietary guidelines, making it broadly acceptable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or weight. However, it provides no positive nutritional value and should not displace water or nutrient-dense beverages. Individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU) must avoid this product due to its aspartame content, which is a source of phenylalanine. The product is widely available in Singapore and globally, commonly consumed as a mixer, a refreshment, or a lower-calorie alternative to regular carbonated soft drinks.

All core macros and secondary nutrients are confirmed exact matches. GI assigned as nominal value of 1 (low category) based on peer-reviewed analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008) for diet carbonated beverages with negligible carbohydrate.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — 7 Up Free is a carbonated water-based beverage with no protein-containing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — no fat-containing ingredients are present. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — artificial sweeteners (aspartame, acesulfame K) replace all sugar and contribute negligible carbohydrate mass. All three confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D06020002). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol are all 0.0 by product formulation as no fat-containing ingredients are present. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this commercial sugar-free carbonated beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D06020002 as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central does not carry a directly matching branded entry with full micronutrient panel. Glycemic index is assigned a nominal value of 1 (low) based on analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008) for diet carbonated beverages; the beverage contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 ml and produces no measurable glycaemic response. GL per 330 ml serving is 0.0. Individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU) must avoid this product due to aspartame content. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input is preserved as the product contains zero net carbohydrates. Nutrient source organisation explicitly identified as Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore, an approved primary regional source per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1.
